sections.1. Introduction:
Treatment of established AKI is largely supportive. In severe cases, RRT becomes necessary. RRT should be started for refractory hyperkalaemia, severe metabolic acidosis, uraemic complications, or fluid overload unresponsive to conservative management.

A central objective in RRT management is to minimise adverse events while optimising treatment efficacy. Anticoagulation plays a pivotal role: all extracorporeal therapies require adequate anticoagulation because contact between blood and the artificial circuit surface activates the coagulation cascade.

Inadequate anticoagulation → premature filter clotting, treatment interruption, blood loss, reduced dialysis efficiency.
Excessive anticoagulation → bleeding complications, metabolic disturbances (citrate accumulation), increased management complexity.
sections.2. Anticoagulation:
Blood circulation through the extracorporeal circuit activates platelets and coagulation pathways, forming microthrombi that deposit within the filter and circuit, ultimately impairing blood flow and causing circuit failure. Effective anticoagulation is required to maximise circuit lifespan.

Aim: maintain patency of the extracorporeal circuit while minimising patient complications.

Important: ineffective anticoagulation is not the only reason for early circuit clotting. Other causes include high filtration fraction and compromised vascular access. CRRT settings (pre-dilution, low FF) can reduce the need for anticoagulation. Early filter clotting should not automatically lead to increased anticoagulation dose.

Circuit clotting vs membrane clogging:
• Clotting = thrombus formation somewhere in the circuit, including fibre lumen. Translates into elevated pre-filter and venous pressures.
• Clogging = saturation of membrane pores, representing decreased filter exchange capacity. In CVVHD → decreased dialysate saturation. In CVVH/CVVHDF → increased transmembrane pressure (TMP).

TMP is calculated (not measured):
TMP = (return pressure + pre-filter pressure) / 2 − effluent pressure
sections.2.1 Regional Anticoagulation:
To minimise complications of systemic anticoagulation, strategies have been developed to restrict anticoagulation to the extracorporeal circuit = regional anticoagulation.

Recommended by KDIGO guidelines for critically ill patients.

Principle: administer anticoagulant (citrate) at the beginning of the circuit, reverse it at the end.
sections.2.1.1 Regional Citrate Anticoagulation:
Mechanism: Citrate has high affinity for calcium. It binds ionised (free) calcium, forming citrate-calcium complexes (CCC). Since calcium is a mandatory cofactor for most coagulation cascade enzymes, reducing plasma calcium below 0.35 mmol/l results in anticoagulation.

Calcium chloride must be administered to compensate for calcium losses in the effluent.

Metabolism of CCC: occurs at the cellular level (Krebs cycle). Under normal conditions, citrate half-life ≈ 5 minutes. The process generates energy (2.48 kJ or 593 calories per mmol of citrate) and releases sodium and calcium ions.

Acid-base effect: net effect of CCC metabolism leads to an alkalotic load (through Na+ release and increased Strong Ion Difference).

Evidence: Several RCTs and meta-analyses (Gattas 2015, Stucker 2015, Bai 2015) demonstrated that RCA vs heparin was associated with:
• Increased filter lifespan
• Decreased need for blood transfusions

RCA is the KDIGO-recommended first-line anticoagulation strategy for CRRT (KDIGO 2012).
sections.2.1.1.1 Caveats:
RCA is safe with fewer complications than systemic heparin, but if inadequately managed can cause electrolyte and acid-base complications. A strict protocol must be followed.

Potential complications:
1. Citrate overload: net citrate administration exceeds alkalotic requirements → metabolic alkalosis. Easily managed.
2. Citrate accumulation: body unable to metabolise all CCC (due to shock, mitochondrial blockage, severe liver failure) → increased calcium substitution needs, hypocalcaemia if severe, metabolic acidosis. Suspect when total/ionised calcium ratio >2.5. Incidence 1–3%. Severe cases require therapy interruption.
3. Hypomagnesaemia: citrate chelates Mg²⁺ → Mg losses through effluent. Most protocols account for this; daily Mg monitoring important.

Mandatory monitoring:
• Post-filter (circuit) ionised calcium: target 0.2–0.35 mmol/l. Adjust citrate-to-blood-flow ratio.
• Systemic ionised calcium: target 1.1–1.2 mmol/l. Adjust calcium-to-effluent ratio.
• Total/ionised calcium ratio: >2.5 indicates citrate accumulation (non-metabolised CCC increases non-ionised fraction).
• Magnesium levels: monitor daily.

Protocol adherence is critical. Each protocol has specific advantages/limitations depending on CRRT modality, available solutions, and device.
sections.2.2 Systemic Anticoagulation:
Historically the default strategy; now an alternative when citrate is contraindicated.

Commonly administered as continuous infusion of unfractionated heparin (UFH). LMWH can be used but kinetics during AKI/RRT are unpredictable.

Balancing act: circuit patency vs systemic bleeding risk.

Monitoring: aPTT (predicts filter coagulation risk and haemorrhage risk).
Targets with UFH: aPTT 1.5–2× baseline, or anti-Xa 0.3–0.6 UI/ml.
Dosing: bolus followed by continuous infusion 800–1200 UI/hr. Can be given in-circuit or systemically.

Complications:
• Increased systemic bleeding risk (intracerebral, haematomas, surgical wounds)
• Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT)

Often sub-therapeutic due to:
• High bleeding risk (thrombocytopenia, hepatic dysfunction, post-surgical, TBI, malignant hypertension)
• Abnormal coagulation tests (prolonged aPTT/INR — often not reflecting actual bleeding risk)
• Frequent interruptions for procedures

Result: filter lifespan with heparin mostly <20–30 hours in real life.

Alternatives (e.g. argatroban, danaparoid): reserved for HIT patients. Require specific monitoring and protocols.
sections.2.3 Other regional anticoagulation metho:
Rarely used in modern practice:

1. Heparin/protamine: heparin administered at circuit start, reversed by protamine before blood returns to patient.
2. Prostacyclin: a platelet antiaggregant used as a heparin-sparing molecule during CRRT.
sections.2.4 Non-pharmacological approaches:
Current evidence supports citrate over no anticoagulation. Avoiding anticoagulation exposes patients to higher circuit clotting risk and more frequent filter changes.

When anticoagulation is contraindicated or as adjunct: non-pharmacological approaches can help prolong filter lifespan. However, these strategies are less extensively studied than pharmacological interventions.

Approaches include: strategic modality selection, blood flow optimisation, catheter site/type choice, and filter selection.
sections.2.4.1 Minimisation of haemoconcentration:
Haemoconcentration is a major factor leading to filter clotting. Particularly important during haemofiltration (convective). Almost insignificant in diffusive modalities (except with very high fluid removal).

In CVVH mode, haemoconcentration estimated by filtration fraction (FF).
FF should be maintained below 25%.

Strategies to reduce FF:
• Pre-dilution fluid replacement
• High blood flow rates
sections.2.4.2 Other strategies:
Additional strategies to optimise circuit lifespan:
• Ensuring adequate catheter performance (poor access → interruptions → microthrombus formation)
• Reducing blood–air contact (minimise exposure in bubble trap)
• Filter selection: larger membrane surface areas, shorter/more numerous hollow fibres, flat-sheet designs, heparin-coated membranes (e.g. AN69ST)
• Saline flushes and online pre-dilution
• Device-integrated strategies: automated adjustments (e.g. reduced blood flow in response to low access pressures)
• Prompt response to alarms associated with blood flow interruptions

questions.question: Which statement best describes the difference between circuit clotting and membrane clogging during RRT?
options: A: Clotting refers to pore saturation; clogging refers to thrombus formation in the circuit
B: Clotting is thrombus formation in the circuit causing elevated pressures; clogging is pore saturation reducing exchange capacity
C: Both terms describe the same phenomenon but in different circuit locations
D: Clotting increases transmembrane pressure; clogging increases pre-filter pressure
E: Clogging only occurs in CVVHD; clotting only occurs in CVVH
questions.explanation: Clotting = thrombus formation in the circuit (including fibre lumen), causing elevated pre-filter and venous pressures. Clogging = saturation of membrane pores, decreasing exchange capacity. In CVVH/CVVHDF, clogging increases TMP; in CVVHD, it causes decreased dialysate saturation.

questions.question: A patient on CVVH has the following circuit pressures: pre-filter pressure 180 mmHg, return pressure 120 mmHg, effluent pressure −80 mmHg. What is the transmembrane pressure (TMP)?
questions.explanation: TMP = (return pressure + pre-filter pressure) / 2 − effluent pressure = (120 + 180) / 2 − (−80) = 150 + 80 = 230 mmHg.

questions.question: According to KDIGO guidelines, what is the recommended first-line anticoagulation strategy for CRRT in critically ill patients?
options: E: No anticoagulation with saline flushes
questions.explanation: KDIGO recommends regional citrate anticoagulation (RCA) as the first-line strategy for CRRT. Multiple RCTs and meta-analyses have demonstrated that RCA is associated with increased filter lifespan and decreased transfusion needs compared to heparin.

questions.question: What is the primary mechanism by which citrate achieves regional anticoagulation in the extracorporeal circuit?
options: B: Chelation of ionised calcium, reducing it below 0.35 mmol/l
C: Inhibition of platelet aggregation via COX pathway
E: Antagonism of vitamin K-dependent clotting factors
questions.explanation: Citrate binds ionised (free) calcium with high affinity, forming citrate-calcium complexes (CCC). Since calcium is a mandatory cofactor for most coagulation cascade enzymes, reducing plasma calcium below 0.35 mmol/l in the circuit results in effective anticoagulation.

questions.question: During regional citrate anticoagulation, what is the target range for post-filter (circuit) ionised calcium?
questions.explanation: Post-filter ionised calcium should be maintained between 0.20 and 0.35 mmol/l to ensure adequate anticoagulation within the circuit. This is achieved by adjusting the citrate-to-blood-flow ratio on the CRRT device.

questions.question: During RCA therapy, what systemic ionised calcium level should be maintained?
questions.explanation: Systemic ionised calcium should be maintained at normal levels (1.1–1.2 mmol/l) throughout RCA therapy. This is ensured by adjusting the calcium replacement infusion (calcium-to-effluent ratio).

questions.question: During RCA, a rising total/ionised calcium ratio is observed. Above which threshold is citrate accumulation highly suspected?
questions.explanation: A total/ionised calcium ratio > 2.5 is highly indicative of citrate accumulation. Non-metabolised citrate-calcium complexes (CCC) continue to circulate, increasing the non-ionised fraction of total calcium while ionised calcium may be normal or low.

questions.question: A patient on RCA develops metabolic alkalosis with stable ionised calcium levels. Which condition is the most likely explanation?
questions.explanation: Citrate overload occurs when net citrate administration exceeds the body's alkalotic requirements, resulting in metabolic alkalosis. It is easily managed. In contrast, citrate accumulation (inability to metabolise CCC) presents with metabolic acidosis and hypocalcaemia.

questions.question: When using unfractionated heparin for CRRT anticoagulation, what is the commonly recommended aPTT target?
options: E: Normal aPTT is the target
questions.explanation: With UFH for CRRT, the target aPTT is 1.5–2.0 times baseline, or alternatively an anti-Xa activity between 0.3 and 0.6 UI/ml. A bolus followed by continuous infusion of 800–1200 UI/hr is usually necessary.

questions.question: What is the approximate half-life of citrate under normal metabolic conditions?
questions.explanation: Under normal conditions, citrate's half-life is approximately 5 minutes. Citrate-calcium complexes are metabolised at the cellular level in the Krebs cycle, generating energy (2.48 kJ or 593 calories per mmol) and releasing sodium and calcium ions.

questions.question: After metabolism of citrate-calcium complexes (CCC), what is the net acid-base effect?
options: A: Acidotic load due to bicarbonate consumption
C: Alkalotic load through Na⁺ release and increased Strong Ion Difference
D: Respiratory alkalosis via CO₂ generation
E: Lactic acidosis from anaerobic metabolism
questions.explanation: CCC metabolism releases Na⁺ ions and increases the Strong Ion Difference (SID), resulting in a net alkalotic load. This is why citrate overload presents as metabolic alkalosis.

questions.question: During CVVH, what is the recommended maximum filtration fraction (FF) to minimise haemoconcentration and clotting risk?
questions.explanation: The filtration fraction should be maintained below 25% to minimise haemoconcentration. This can be achieved by using pre-dilution fluid replacement and high blood flow rates. Haemoconcentration is a major factor in filter clotting, especially in convective modalities.

questions.question: A patient on CRRT develops heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT). Which anticoagulant is an appropriate alternative?
options: A: Increased dose of unfractionated heparin
questions.explanation: Argatroban (and danaparoid) are alternatives reserved for patients with HIT. They require specific monitoring and protocols. LMWH should be avoided in HIT due to cross-reactivity risk. Increasing UFH dose would worsen the condition.

questions.question: Apart from inadequate anticoagulation, which of the following is a recognised cause of early circuit clotting during CRRT?
questions.explanation: High filtration fraction and compromised vascular access are important non-anticoagulation causes of early circuit clotting. Excessive pre-dilution and high blood flow actually reduce clotting risk by lowering the filtration fraction.

questions.question: Which membrane type has unfractionated heparin bound to the polymer surface, potentially improving circuit lifespan without systemic anticoagulation?
questions.explanation: AN69ST is a heparin-coated membrane where unfractionated heparin is bound to the polymer surface. This is one of several filter-selection strategies to improve circuit lifespan in a non-pharmacological manner.

questions.question: Regarding circuit clotting and membrane clogging during RRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Clotting refers to thrombus formation in the extracorporeal circuit, including the fibre lumen

statements.label: B
statements.text: Clogging is associated with elevated pre-filter and venous pressures

statements.label: C
statements.text: In CVVH or CVVHDF, clogging typically causes an increase in transmembrane pressure (TMP)

statements.label: D
statements.text: In CVVHD, clogging results in decreased saturation of the dialysate

questions.explanation: Clotting = thrombus → elevated pre-filter and venous pressures (not clogging). Clogging = pore saturation → in CVVH/CVVHDF: increased TMP; in CVVHD: decreased dialysate saturation (incomplete equilibrium).

questions.question: Regarding potential complications of regional citrate anticoagulation, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Citrate overload is associated with metabolic alkalosis

statements.label: B
statements.text: Citrate accumulation is associated with metabolic alkalosis

statements.label: C
statements.text: The reported incidence of citrate accumulation ranges from 1% to 3%

statements.label: D
statements.text: Hypomagnesaemia may occur because citrate chelates magnesium ions

questions.explanation: Citrate overload → metabolic alkalosis (excess alkalotic load). Citrate accumulation → metabolic acidosis (inability to metabolise CCC, with hypocalcaemia and increased Ca substitution needs). These are distinct entities. Citrate also chelates Mg²⁺, causing losses through effluent.

questions.question: Regarding mandatory monitoring during regional citrate anticoagulation, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Post-filter ionised calcium should be maintained at 1.1–1.2 mmol/l

statements.label: B
statements.text: A total/ionised calcium ratio > 2.5 is indicative of citrate accumulation

statements.label: C
statements.text: Systemic ionised calcium should be maintained at 0.20–0.35 mmol/l

statements.label: D
statements.text: Magnesium levels should be monitored daily during RCA

questions.explanation: Post-filter iCa target is 0.20–0.35 mmol/l (circuit anticoagulation level). Systemic iCa target is 1.1–1.2 mmol/l (normal physiological level). The two values are frequently confused — a classic exam trap.

questions.question: Regarding systemic anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin for CRRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Low molecular weight heparin has predictable kinetics during AKI and RRT

statements.label: B
statements.text: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) is a recognised complication

statements.label: C
statements.text: In real-life practice, heparin anticoagulation typically achieves filter lifespan over 48 hours

statements.label: D
statements.text: The alternative monitoring target with UFH is anti-Xa activity of 0.3–0.6 UI/ml

questions.explanation: LMWH kinetics are unpredictable during AKI/RRT. HIT is a known complication of UFH. Real-life filter lifespan with heparin is mostly under 20–30 hours (due to frequent sub-therapeutic dosing, interruptions). Anti-Xa 0.3–0.6 UI/ml is the alternative target to aPTT 1.5–2× baseline.

questions.question: Regarding non-pharmacological approaches to anticoagulation during CRRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Current evidence supports that citrate anticoagulation is superior to no anticoagulation

statements.label: B
statements.text: Minimising blood–air contact in the bubble trap can decrease clotting risk

statements.label: C
statements.text: Reducing blood flow rate is a recommended strategy to prolong filter lifespan

statements.label: D
statements.text: Prompt response to machine alarms helps prevent circuit clotting

questions.explanation: Citrate is superior to no anticoagulation (evidence-based). Reducing blood–air contact helps. High (not low) blood flow rates reduce haemoconcentration and clotting. Rapid alarm response prevents blood flow interruptions that promote clotting.

questions.question: Regarding citrate metabolism and its physiological effects, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Citrate-calcium complexes are metabolised in the Krebs cycle at the cellular level

statements.label: B
statements.text: Citrate metabolism generates approximately 593 calories per mmol

statements.label: C
statements.text: Citrate metabolism releases potassium and calcium ions

statements.label: D
statements.text: The net acid-base effect of CCC metabolism is an alkalotic load

questions.explanation: CCC are metabolised in the Krebs cycle, generating 2.48 kJ (593 cal) per mmol. The process releases sodium and calcium ions (not potassium). The net effect is alkalotic (Na⁺ release increases Strong Ion Difference).

questions.question: Regarding the comparison of regional citrate anticoagulation (RCA) versus systemic heparin for CRRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: RCA is associated with increased filter lifespan compared to heparin

statements.label: B
statements.text: RCA is associated with increased need for blood transfusions compared to heparin

statements.label: C
statements.text: RCA is the KDIGO-recommended first-line anticoagulation for CRRT

statements.label: D
statements.text: RCA is associated with a higher risk of systemic bleeding compared to heparin

questions.explanation: RCTs/meta-analyses show RCA has increased filter lifespan and decreased (not increased) transfusion needs vs heparin. RCA is KDIGO first-line. Systemic bleeding risk is lower with RCA (regional, not systemic effect) vs heparin.

questions.question: Regarding filtration fraction (FF) and haemoconcentration during CRRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: High filtration fraction promotes haemoconcentration and filter clotting

statements.label: B
statements.text: Pre-dilution fluid replacement helps reduce the filtration fraction

statements.label: C
statements.text: Haemoconcentration is equally important in diffusive and convective modalities

statements.label: D
statements.text: FF should be maintained below 25%

questions.explanation: High FF → haemoconcentration → clotting. Pre-dilution reduces FF. Haemoconcentration is primarily important in convective (haemofiltration) modalities; it is almost insignificant in diffusive modalities except with very high fluid removal. Target: FF < 25%.

questions.question: Regarding alternative regional anticoagulation methods for CRRT, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In heparin/protamine regional anticoagulation, protamine is administered at the beginning of the circuit

statements.label: B
statements.text: Prostacyclin is a platelet antiaggregant used as a heparin-sparing molecule during CRRT

statements.label: C
statements.text: Heparin/protamine regional anticoagulation is the current standard of care for CRRT

statements.label: D
statements.text: Danaparoid is classified as a regional anticoagulation method

questions.explanation: Heparin is given at circuit start, protamine at the end (to reverse heparin before blood returns). Prostacyclin is a platelet antiaggregant used as a heparin-sparing agent. Both methods are rarely used in modern practice. Danaparoid is a systemic anticoagulant (alternative for HIT), not a regional method.

questions.question: Regarding citrate accumulation during regional citrate anticoagulation, assess each statement:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Citrate accumulation occurs when the body cannot metabolise all citrate-calcium complexes

statements.label: B
statements.text: Shock and severe liver failure are risk factors for citrate accumulation

statements.label: C
statements.text: Citrate accumulation is associated with metabolic alkalosis

statements.label: D
statements.text: In severe cases, citrate accumulation may require interruption of RCA therapy

questions.explanation: Citrate accumulation = inability to metabolise CCC (due to shock, mitochondrial blockage, severe liver failure). It causes metabolic acidosis (not alkalosis — that is citrate overload), hypocalcaemia, and increased calcium substitution needs. Severe cases require therapy interruption. Incidence 1–3%.
